feature_name: Dual-mode operation (Root Complex or End Point)
description: The controller supports operation as either a PCIe Root Complex or a PCIe End Point, selectable via bootstrap pins and overridable in device status. In RC mode it initiates configuration cycles and manages downstream enumeration. In EP mode it exposes Type 0 configuration space to a host and responds to configuration requests. The choice affects capabilities like IO window availability and certain registers’ behavior. Mode selection is fundamental and must be finalized before link training begins.
evidence: PCIESSMODE[1:0] (bootstrap); DEVSTAT override (spec Functional/Mode); CFG_SETUP[CFG_TYPE] for Type0/1
dependencies: Requires PSC power enable and DEVSTAT[PCIESSEN]=1; proper SerDes refclk present; finalize before CMD_STATUS[LTSSM_EN]=1
Remarks: Set desired mode at boot; changing after link-up requires reset and retraining.

description: The link supports 2.5 GT/s (Gen1) and 5.0 GT/s (Gen2) data rates. Negotiation occurs during training with control via Link Control 2 settings. Proper equalization and PHY configuration are necessary for stable Gen2 operation. Common-clock and SSC decisions influence reliable Gen2 performance. Verify negotiated speed post-training.
evidence: LINK_CTRL2/GEN2 controls; Port Logic GEN2 registers (PL_GEN2); LINK_STAT_CTRL
dependencies: Stable refclk and SerDes PLL lock; correct COMMON_CLK_CFG when shared clock; proper board routing
Remarks: Default to Gen1 for bring-up, then enable Gen2 after link proves stable.

feature_name: Link width x1/x2 with lane reversal and polarity inversion
description: The controller operates with one or two lanes depending on board design. Automatic lane reversal and receive polarity inversion permit flexible routing. Dynamic width conversion supports down-training if only one lane is detected. Status registers expose active lanes and alignment. Ensure board routing and SerDes settings match intended width.
evidence: PCS_STATUS lanes enabled; Port Logic/Debug lane state; SerDes CFG fields
dependencies: SerDes PLL locked; correct lane enablement in SERDES_CFG0/1; compatible partner device
Remarks: Allow automatic reversal; do not force settings unless required by board constraints.

description: Outbound mappings translate internal addresses into PCIe memory/IO/config spaces. Windows are configured using an index and high bits registers with a common size register. Correct setup is required for DMA or CPU-initiated PCIe transactions. Enabling outbound translation activates the programmed windows. Accesses must be non-burst and non-cacheable.
evidence: OB_OFFSET_INDEXn/OB_OFFSETn_HI at 0x200+; OB_SIZE at 0x30; CMD_STATUS[OB_XLT_EN] at 0x04
dependencies: Program target base/size first; enable after configuration; ensure link is up before generating TLPs
Remarks: Validate that targets exist; avoid remote config/IO when link is down to prevent bus hangs.

description: Inbound translation defines internal memory regions accessible from the PCIe side. Up to four regions can be exposed by programming BAR selection, start, and offset registers. These mappings are required for host access to device memory in EP mode or peer writes in RC systems. Translation must be enabled in the command/status register. Ensure size and alignment conform to host expectations.
evidence: IB_BARn 0x300+; IB_STARTn_LO/HI 0x304/0x308+; IB_OFFSETn 0x30C+; CMD_STATUS[IB_XLT_EN]
dependencies: BAR apertures consistent with host enumeration; translators enabled; non-cacheable mappings
Remarks: Use minimal apertures consistent with performance to limit attack surface and decode complexity.

description: The application endianness of internal interfaces is configurable. Software must select the appropriate byte ordering to match the system architecture. Misconfiguration can corrupt descriptor and data interpretations. Endianness should be set before enabling translators and starting traffic. Once traffic is active, changes require quiescence and revalidation.
evidence: ENDIAN register at 0x38
dependencies: Set prior to enabling OB/IB translation and before DMA or CPU transactions
Remarks: For big-endian systems, validate that BAR-accessible application registers are consistently interpreted by the host.

feature_name: TLP attributes: Relaxed Ordering and No Snoop
description: The controller can mark outbound transactions with relaxed ordering and no-snoop attributes. Enabling these can improve throughput on systems tolerant of reordering and without snoop-coherent paths. Some hosts or devices may ignore or restrict these attributes. Use with awareness of memory consistency requirements. Always measure effects on latency and bandwidth.
evidence: TLPCFG at 0x10 (RELAXED, NO_SNOOP)
dependencies: System interconnect must tolerate reordering; cache policy must allow no-snoop
Remarks: Start with defaults off; enable selectively for large sequential DMA workloads.

description: The controller supports legacy INTA/B/C/D signaling and maps them to SoC interrupt inputs. Events are acknowledged via an end-of-interrupt write with the corresponding vector. Proper masking and clearing of raw and latched status are required. Legacy mode is useful for compatibility with devices lacking MSI. Latency may be higher than MSI in heavy interrupt loads.
evidence: IRQ_EOI at 0x50; INTx status/enable 0x64–0x120
dependencies: SoC interrupt router configured; vectors mapped; status cleared with W1C where applicable
Remarks: Prefer MSI when available; reserve INTx for fallback and early bring-up diagnostics.

description: As a Root Complex, the controller receives and services MSIs from endpoints. MSI vectors are grouped across enable and status blocks, with write-one-to-clear semantics for pending bits. Software must route and enable only the vectors in use. The EOI mechanism is used to signal interrupt completion. Ensure MSI capability is enabled on endpoints during enumeration.
evidence: MSI*_IRQ_ENABLE_SET/CLR 0x90–0x17C; MSI_IRQ at 0x54 (status)
dependencies: Enumeration enables MSI on endpoints; SoC interrupt mapping done; status cleared
Remarks: Batch-handle vectors per group to minimize register traffic in high-rate scenarios.

description: In End Point mode the controller can generate MSI to signal events to a host. Software programs the local MSI capability and writes to the MSI trigger register to raise an interrupt. Host enumeration must allocate and map MSI vectors for the function. Proper timing and ordering ensure that MSI signals correspond to completed DMA or state changes. Use MSI over INTx when supported by the host.
evidence: Local MSI capability in config space; MSI_IRQ at 0x54 (EP generation)
dependencies: MSI capability programmed; host assigned and enabled vectors; BAR and IB regions valid
Remarks: Coalesce interrupts to reduce host overhead while maintaining latency targets.

description: The module supports device D-states via the Power Management capability. Active State Power Management provides L0s and L1 link states for energy savings. Software can request PM events such as turn-off or PME as appropriate for role. L2/L3 ready signaling exists but L2 link state is not supported by this module. Careful entry/exit handling avoids data loss and surprise down conditions.
evidence: PMCAP 0x1040; PM_CTL_STAT 0x1044; PMCMD 0x20; PMCFG 0x24; LINK_STAT_CTRL (ASPM)
dependencies: Stable link; quiesced traffic before low-power transitions; partner supports ASPM policy
Remarks: Enable ASPM only after verifying board and partner clocking; monitor latency impact.

description: Dedicated interrupts report power management and reset-related events. Software must enable, service, and clear raw and masked status to maintain responsiveness. These events include PME, turn-off acknowledgments, and link reset requests. Proper handling coordinates orderly shutdowns and recoveries. Neglect can stall power transitions or leave stale status latched.
evidence: PMRST_IRQ_STATUS_RAW 0x1D0; PMRST_IRQ_STATUS 0x1D4; enables 0x1D8/0x1DC
dependencies: SoC interrupt mapping; PM sequences implemented; status clear ordering respected
Remarks: Log PM/reset causes for field diagnostics and to refine low-power policies.

description: Advanced Error Reporting extends visibility into correctable and uncorrectable errors. End-to-end CRC generation and check help detect TLP corruption across the fabric. Software should enable logging and thresholds consistent with deployment reliability goals. On error, capture logs before clearing to preserve root cause. Some partners may not implement AER fully, so interoperability testing is advised.
evidence: PCIe AER extended capability; ECRC enable in caps (DEV_CAP2/DEV_STAT_CTRL2)
dependencies: AER capability present; interrupts routed; sufficient log buffers
Remarks: Integrate error counters into health monitoring and trigger recovery flows on fatal errors.

description: The PHY requires programming of chip-level SerDes PLL controls followed by lock verification. Writes to these registers may require an unlock sequence using a device-specific kicker. After enabling the PLL, the module specifies wait times before lock is guaranteed. Software must poll the lock bit and only proceed when asserted. Lane-specific status helps diagnose signal integrity or configuration issues.
evidence: PCIE_SERDES_CFGPLL (e.g., 0x02620358); PCIE_SERDES_STS (e.g., 0x0262015C) [LOCK]; Kicker unlock
dependencies: Stable reference clock applied; kicker unlocked; observe post-enable delays
Remarks: Abstract SerDes programming into a reusable routine shared across KeyStone derivatives.

feature_name: Link training control and LTSSM monitoring
description: Software enables the Link Training and Status State Machine to initiate negotiation. Until translators and config are ready, training should remain disabled. After enabling, status registers reveal the current LTSSM state, with L0 indicating an operational link. Failure to reach L0 suggests clocking, cabling, or configuration issues. Automated bring-up scripts should gate enumeration on successful L0 detection.
evidence: CMD_STATUS[LTSSM_EN] at 0x04; Port Logic DEBUG0/1 (LTSSM state; L0=0x11)
dependencies: SerDes lock achieved; translators configured; interrupts routed for link events
Remarks: Add timeouts and retries around training to handle marginal links gracefully.

description: A software-initiated hot reset can be issued to downstream ports. The reset command bit is write-one-to-set, and traffic must be flushed prior to assertion. Adequate delays after reset are required before resuming transactions. Proper handling ensures endpoints re-enumerate cleanly. Misuse can lead to device timeouts or link hangs.
evidence: RSTCMD[INIT_RST] at 0x14; RSTCMD[FLUSH_N] semantics; timing guidance (≥100 ms)
dependencies: RC role; traffic quiesced; timers enforced before access
Remarks: Integrate hot reset into error recovery procedures with exponential backoff.

description: The Root Complex can issue configuration and IO cycles using dedicated outbound windows. Software selects Type 0 or Type 1 configuration targets through the setup register. A small IO window is provided for legacy devices. Accesses must be single 32-bit transactions with no bursting. Attempts while the link is down or with no target present can hang the bus.
evidence: CFG_SETUP at 0x08; IOBASE at 0x0C (4 KB IO window); non-bursting rule
dependencies: Link trained to L0; targets discovered; windows configured before use
Remarks: Guard all remote accesses with link-up checks and target presence verification.

description: Outbound and inbound translators are gated by enable bits to prevent unintended accesses. This allows staged configuration and safe bring-up. Clearing enables quickly isolates faults during debug. Proper sequencing reduces exposure to malformed or misrouted TLPs. Always disable translators before performing major remaps.
evidence: CMD_STATUS[OB_XLT_EN, IB_XLT_EN] at 0x04
dependencies: OB/IB tables valid; link state known; interrupt handlers ready
Remarks: Use feature flags in software to centralize translator state control.

description: Per-lane PCS and SerDes configuration and status are memory-mapped in the application space. These registers offer fine-grained control for debug, including resets, delays, and error injection. Status exposes lane enable states and operational readiness. While powerful, these controls should not be altered in production without validation. They are invaluable for SI bring-up and margining.
evidence: PCS_CFG0 0x380; PCS_CFG1 0x384; PCS_STATUS 0x388; SERDES_CFG0 0x390; SERDES_CFG1 0x394
dependencies: Kicker unlock if required; SerDes powered; expert-only usage
Remarks: Wrap low-level tweaks behind guarded build flags and runtime service hooks.

description: The module interfaces to the SoC with wide internal buses for high throughput. Inbound and outbound paths are optimized for 128-bit data movement. However, controller-side special address spaces mandate single 32-bit, non-burst, non-cacheable accesses. Violating these rules can lead to protocol errors or deadlocks. DMA paths should use memory-mapped windows rather than controller register spaces.
evidence: Functional overview; Access rules for app/local/remote config/IO spaces
dependencies: MMU/cache attributes set to strongly-ordered or device; drivers enforce access width
Remarks: Centralize MMIO helpers that enforce 32-bit non-burst semantics for these regions.

description: When a common reference clock is shared with the link partner, software should set the common-clock bit. Spread-spectrum clocking improves EMI but requires compatible clocking on both ends. Mismatched settings can degrade link stability, especially at Gen2 rates. Proper configuration maximizes interoperability. Always confirm negotiated settings in link status registers.
evidence: LINK_STAT_CTRL[COMMON_CLK_CFG]; PL_GEN2 notes on Tx swing and SSC
dependencies: Knowledge of board clock topology; stable refclk; SerDes configured accordingly
Remarks: Document clocking topology per platform and validate during manufacturing test.

description: A well-defined sequence brings the controller to operational state as RC. Power and enable the module and configure SerDes before attempting link training. Program endianness, translators, and interrupts with link training disabled. After enabling LTSSM, wait for L0 before enumerating and enabling endpoint features like MSI. Retry and timeout policies should account for slow devices and reset recovery.
evidence: Bring-up steps list in spec summary (steps 1–11)
dependencies: PSC power; SerDes lock; translators configured; interrupt routing; enumeration code
Remarks: Automate sequence with checkpoints and structured error handling for robustness.

description: End Point bring-up configures BARs, capabilities, and inbound windows prior to training. SerDes must be configured and locked before enabling LTSSM. After link-up, the host performs configuration cycles which the endpoint must service. MSI should be enabled if supported and mapped. Address translators must reflect host-assigned apertures.
evidence: EP steps in spec summary (items 1–5)
dependencies: BAR sizes chosen; IB/OB programmed; MSI capability; SerDes lock; LTSSM gating
Remarks: Defer DMA until host completes configuration and memory windows are validated.

description: The module does not provide hardware hot-plug support and does not support the L2 link state. Software should avoid relying on these features and implement alternatives where needed. Power management should target L0s/L1 only. Recovery paths must consider the absence of L2 transitions. Documentation should reflect these architectural limits.
evidence: Functional notes: Unsupported features list (no L2, no hot plug HW)
dependencies: System design avoids L2 and hot-plug dependencies; PM policies bounded to L0s/L1
Remarks: Design platform-level solutions for device presence changes if required.

feature_name: Payload and read request size limits
description: The controller enforces specific maximum payload and request sizes in each direction. Outbound payloads are limited to 128 bytes, while inbound payloads and remote read request sizes allow up to 256 bytes. These constraints influence DMA descriptor sizing and batching strategies. Software must program matching values in capability registers during enumeration. Exceeding limits can reduce performance or cause protocol rejections.
evidence: Functional capabilities: payload 128B OB, 256B IB, read request 256B; DEV_CAP/DEV_CAP2 fields
dependencies: Enumeration code negotiates MPS/MRRS within limits; endpoints configured accordingly
Remarks: Tune DMA chunking to align with MPS/MRRS and IOMMU boundaries for best throughput.

description: Inbound transactions can carry priority and privilege identifiers for system QoS. Software can set master priority levels and privilege IDs to integrate with SoC fabric policies. Misconfiguration could starve or overload other masters. The feature is optional but useful in shared-memory environments. Values should be audited in safety-critical systems.
evidence: PRIORITY register at 0x3C
dependencies: Coordination with SoC QoS and security frameworks; policy defined
Remarks: Expose tunables via device-tree or platform config with conservative defaults.

description: Activity status and diagnostic controls provide insight during operation. These registers can indicate ongoing transactions, error states, or internal conditions. Careful use assists with performance tuning and troubleshooting. Overuse of intrusive diagnostics can perturb timing. Access should follow non-burst rules.
evidence: ACT_STATUS at 0x28; DIAG_CTRL at 0x34
dependencies: Non-burst MMIO helpers; debug only in low impact windows
Remarks: Sample counters periodically and correlate with interrupt logs for actionable insights.

description: An I/O space window is provided for legacy device access when acting as RC. The aperture is small and must be used cautiously. Many modern endpoints do not implement I/O space and prefer memory-mapped access. Access rules still require single 32-bit transactions. Software should validate device capabilities before relying on I/O space.
evidence: IOBASE at 0x0C (4 KB window); CFG_SETUP Type1->Type0 targeting
dependencies: RC mode only; enumerated endpoints advertising I/O BARs
Remarks: Fallback to memory space if I/O BARs are absent or cause frequent stalls.

description: The controller does not support multiple virtual channels or traffic classes beyond defaults. Function-level reset is also not implemented. Software should not advertise or depend on these capabilities. Negotiation during enumeration must reflect these limits. Attempting to use them can cause interoperability problems.
evidence: Functional notes: Unsupported features list (no multi-VC/TC; no FLR)
dependencies: Enumeration logic constrained; capability bits cleared
Remarks: Keep capability advertisement minimal to maximize compatibility across hosts and endpoints.
